ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers raw veal meat; shank-specific sub-code not available; parent code used as defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw veal shank serving is approximately 200 g (bone-in), yielding roughly 130–150 g of edible lean meat after cooking and bone removal.

nutrient_source.title: Veal, shank (fore and hind), separable lean only, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17195)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06030048, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw veal shank (NDB 17195) for secondary nutrient completion. Values are consistent between sources. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB did not report them.

gi_source.title: N/A — Veal shank, raw contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Veal shank is a pure protein-and-fat food with 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; this does not represent a measured GI of zero but rather the absence of a meaningful GI value for a negligible-carbohydrate food. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (84 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium (314 mg/100 g) yielding a sodium-to-potassium ratio of approximately 0.27, well within cardioprotective targets.
hypertension.guidance: Veal shank is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces; its naturally low sodium and meaningful potassium content support blood pressure regulation per DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; high protein (19.15 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability without raising postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Veal shank is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrate and no sugar, does not raise blood glucose, and its high protein content promotes satiety; avoid high-sugar glazes or braising sauces that would add carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.48 g/100 g) with saturated fat of approximately 0.93 g/100 g, well below the 5 g/100 g threshold; cholesterol is moderate at 75 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Veal shank is a lean red meat with low saturated fat content, making it acceptable in a heart-healthy diet when consumed in moderate portions (up to 2–3 times per week) and prepared by braising or poaching without added saturated fats; individuals with established CVD should monitor total red meat intake per AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (bovine/calf) contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit veal shank to no more than 1–2 servings per week per EULAR dietary guidance, and prefer anti-inflammatory protein sources such as oily fish or legumes as primary protein staples.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veal shank, raw, is the bone-in lower leg cut from a young calf (veal), displaying a pale pink to light red colour with a dense, fine-grained muscle texture. The cut contains the central marrow-filled tibia bone surrounded by lean muscle meat with small amounts of intramuscular and external fat. Raw veal shank is nutritionally lean, providing 113 kcal per 100 g, 19.15 g of high-quality complete protein, and only 3.48 g of total fat (of which approximately 0.93 g is saturated fat). It contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it glycaemically neutral. Micronutrient highlights include potassium (314 mg), phosphorus (196 mg), zinc (3.1 mg), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), niacin (6.8 mg), and vitamin B6 (0.36 mg). Sodium is low at 84 mg/100 g. Veal shank is the classic cut used in the Italian dish osso buco, where it is slow-braised in white wine, tomatoes, and aromatics until the collagen-rich connective tissue breaks down into a rich, gelatinous sauce. It is also used in French blanquette de veau and various European braises. Clinically, veal shank is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (zero carbohydrate), hypertension (low sodium, good potassium), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat). As a red meat, it should be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when slaughtered according to Islamic rites.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw veal shank?
Is veal shank good for diabetics?
Is veal shank low in sodium?
What cut is used for osso buco?
Is veal shank suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06030048, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary and micronutrient values cross-referenced and supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw veal shank, an approved primary source. Values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for a lean raw veal cut.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: veal shank is a pure muscle meat with no carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same rationale. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for raw veal). Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: reliable vitamin D data for raw veal shank is not consistently reported in either HPB or USDA SR Legacy as of 2026. Saturated_fat_g (0.93 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.31 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.28 g), and all micronutrient values not present in HPB input were sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17195 (veal shank, separable lean only, raw). Food subgroup corrected from 'Beef' (input) to 'Veal' as veal is a distinct subcategory of bovine meat from young calves. Halal status noted as conditional on slaughter method; dietary tag preserved from input. Kosher status not confirmed and set to false. Veal shank, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g). The glycemic_index field is set to the numeric value 0 as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; this does not represent a measured GI of zero but rather the inapplicability of GI for a negligible-carbohydrate food. gi_category is set to not_applicable.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Veal shank, raw'. No multilingual requirement applies as this is a Western/European generic ingredient, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. G6 is satisfied.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric value 0 (sentinel for not-applicable, as required by validator — glycemic_index must be numeric). gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source fully populated. Food contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; GI is not applicable. The numeric 0 sentinel is documented in notes and gi_source.source_match_notes to prevent misinterpretation as a measured GI value.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for veal shank (FOODON_03311349). FoodEx2 code A0CKP (veal meat, raw) used as defensible parent anchor with gap note explaining shank-specific sub-code unavailability. SNOMED CT 226934003 (Veal, food) provided. No codes invented.

Covers food identity, preparation context (osso buco, blanquette de veau), taste/texture, clinical relevance (diabetes, hypertension, CVD, RA), and key nutrients. Suitable for RAG/LLM training.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis preserved as additional_condition.
